title: Urban planning decisions expose Traveller sites to disproportionate environmental burdens

data_scope.site_count: not stated in available metadata; defined in deposited Recherche Data Gouv record

core_conclusion: In France, designated Traveller sites are systematically placed where environmental burdens are highest: cities with more disamenities are more likely to host a site, and within hosting cities the site is more exposed than any other residential area, including other disadvantaged households. The pattern is consistent with cost-minimization by municipalities and discriminatory preferences, showing how routine urban-planning decisions produce systemic socio-spatial inequality in Europe.
why_it_matters: Provides rare rigorous environmental-justice evidence for a European ethnic minority; identifies urban planning itself as the discrimination mechanism; separates between-city from within-city effects; and names testable mechanisms (cost minimization vs discriminatory preferences) that are directly actionable for siting policy.
why_nature: Links the geometry of urban-planning decisions to environmental injustice and well-being (Nature Cities remit); frames a single national case as a transferable European lesson; highly policy-translatable; rigorous identification on a newly assembled national dataset with explicit mechanism testing.
added_value_to_my_research: Mobility-weighted exposure via GPS trajectories + spatiotemporal KDE for mobile marginalized groups
Explainable ML / MGWR to map spatially varying siting-discrimination effects and counterfactual placements
Joint burden-benefit modeling coupling disamenity exposure with 2SFCA/R2SFCA service & healthcare access
Multi-objective efficiency-equity facility-location for justice-constrained site siting (EV-optimization analogue)
Cross-country transfer via OSM + remote sensing + admin data fused with a geospatial foundation model
